chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Next we are going to cover the common NumPy data types.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: In which we have these commonly used data types.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Now, apart from this, there are many other data types in NumPy.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: But right now we are going to cover the most important ones.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: First of all, our most basic data type is integers.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: In this, we have int32, int64.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Along with that, we also have options of int128 or even we can downsize to int16 or int8.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, in this way, we have a lot of variations of integer data types.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: In terms of the number of bits that they use to store data.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, for that, let's create a NumPy array.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: We are basically talking about commonly used data types.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, first of all, let's create a NumPy array.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: np.array.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: In which we can pass an integer value list.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: And we are going to print array plus array.datatype.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, array is this and array.datatype is integer 64.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Now, here it is 64 or 32.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: I have already said it.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: It depends on our system.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: If we are on a Mac system, then we will generally see 64.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: If we are on a Windows system, depending upon our machine, we can have 64 or 32.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Along with that, we can also have floating values.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: If we convert any value here into a floating point, then our entire array will be converted into a float type.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, this becomes a float 64 array.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, we have different options in the float.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: We have float 32, we have float 64, float 128 and so on.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Now, whenever we are talking about the number of bits in the data types,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: then we know that we have int8, we have int16, we have int32, we have int64, we have int128.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, there are multiple variations of the data types that we have.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Definitely, the smaller data types occupy less memory.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: The bigger data types occupy more memory.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: The smaller data types are more efficient because they occupy less memory.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, if we perform an operation on this data, then it will be slightly faster as compared to this data.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, what we can do is whenever we have to store the data,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: whenever we have to pick the appropriate data type,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: here, automatically, float64 and int64 are being selected for us.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: But if we want, we can also downcast it as per our wish.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: We heard about typecasting.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, when we cast our values in a small data type in typecasting, that is basically downcasting.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, for example, let's suppose we have to store the data of age.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Let's suppose we have a lot of data of employees on which we want to train our machine learning model.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: And we have some parameter values of employees' age.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Now, we know that the maximum value of age will be 100, 120, 150,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: more than 200, the age value cannot be used.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, in this case, instead of using int64, we can use int32 or int16 to actually improve the performance a little bit

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: or maybe save our space.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, in this kind of situation where we already know that how much our expected maximum value or minimum value can go,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: in that case, we can also select the appropriate data types to increase the performance.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Another example of this will be images.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: We know that if we take the example of black and white images,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: then the data of black and white images is actually represented in the form of numbers in our Python code.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Now, we must have thought many times that if we talk about our convolutional neural networks,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: which we will study in detail later,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: then convolutional neural networks basically perform very well on images and video frames.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, whenever we are writing a machine learning or deep learning algorithm that actually works on images or videos,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: then how is that data taking input?

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: We see images as humans.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: But computers only understand numbers.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, what do computers do?

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: We computers have to feed our images in the form of numbers.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, if we take the example of black and white images,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: then we feed black and white images as numbers to the system,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: in which all our images lie within the range of 0 to 255,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: where 0 actually stands for black and 255 stands for white.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: And all the values in between show the degree of grayness.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, any value can be between 0 to 255.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, let's suppose if we want to represent black and white images,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: then we can represent it in the form of a matrix,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: in which every cell has some values that come within this range.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, here it can be 160, here it can be 170, here we can have 0, we can have 5, we can have 10,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: and in this way, every cell has values within this range.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: And this cell represents a black and white image.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, the data of this type of black and white image,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: we know that the value of every pixel cannot be greater than 255.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, we don't actually need such a big data type to store this type of data.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, what we can do is, we can actually downcast our data type in those cases.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Let's look at other data types too.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Along with this, we can create a Boolean array or we can create strings too.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: For example, if we add a string here,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: then our array will become an array of strings and the type will be U32.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Plus, we can also have complex numbers.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Now, we must have studied complex numbers in school, in math.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: We are not going to discuss complex numbers in much detail.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: I will tell you in a very brief way, just for understanding.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Complex numbers are basically a special type of numbers in math,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: which are made up of two different numbers.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Basically, complex numbers have two different parts.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: One is their real part and the other is their imaginary part.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: When we show complex numbers, we show them as a sum of these two numbers.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: For example, if we see 2 plus 3i,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: or 5 plus 8i, or 4 minus 3i,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: then we call these types of numbers as complex numbers.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: The normal number written inside them is the real part of this complex number.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: And the number written with i is their imaginary part.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, i basically stands for the imaginary unit, which is equal to under root minus 1,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: which is not a real value.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, this way, we call these numbers as complex numbers.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Complex numbers are used a lot in math, and in many algorithms.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: That is why complex numbers have a particular type,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: which exists in our numpy.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Because complex numbers are made up of two different numbers.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: This is a different number, and this is a different number.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: That is why, if we have int 64 by default in our system,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: then we will have complex 128 by default in our system.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Why? Because its real part will also be 64,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: and the imaginary part will also be 64.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, by summing both of them, we get the complex number of double size.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, we write complex numbers in this way.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: And the subtraction operations on complex numbers are performed in a different way.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: For example, if we want to add these two complex numbers.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, when we add complex numbers,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: then the real part is added with the real part,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: and the imaginary part is added with the imaginary part.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: And this way, we get the final number.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: In a similar way, we can subtract.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: If we have 5 plus 8i minus,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: we have 2 plus 3i.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, if we subtract the real part from the real part,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: then we get 3.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: And if we subtract the imaginary part from the imaginary part,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: then we get this.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: This will be the final answer.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, these are the basics of complex numbers.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: If we want, we can also create complex numbers.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: For example, I can create a complex number here.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Let's write numpy.array.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: To create a complex number, we basically send a list.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: In which we can write it like this.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: 3 plus 5j.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: We represent it with j.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: And if I want, I can print the array.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: And also the array.detail.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Let's run this.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, we have our complex number.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: By default, these two values are our floating values.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: We have float64, float64.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, our complex type is complex 128.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: If we want, we can create another complex number.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: This is 1.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Let's suppose this is 2.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: In 2, we can have 2 and 3j.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: And if we want, we can perform mathematical operations on complex numbers.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: For example, array1 plus array2.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Basically, when we add both the complex numbers,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: we will get a final result, another complex number.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, we added the real part with the real part.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: And added the imaginary part with the imaginary part.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Similarly, we can also subtract.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, real is subtracted from real.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: And imaginary is subtracted from imaginary.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: These are how our complex numbers work.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Along with that, we also have string and we also have object.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: We have two types of string that we can store.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: One is byte strings that are represented by S.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Another is unicode strings, normal strings that we deal with and are represented by U.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: It is not advisable to use NumPy arrays to store string data or even to store objects.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Because NumPy is optimized for numerical computations.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, we do not prefer NumPy to perform operations on string or object type of data.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: But still, we can create it if we want.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Although, this is not preferable in real-time scenarios.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: There are very few cases in real-life scenarios when we store such data in our NumPy array.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: We have already seen string many times.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: If we want to look at objects, we can create a new array.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Let's suppose we create a list in which there are multiple types of data.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: We have prime.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: We can also have some other values.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Along with that, we can also take floating values.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: If we print array and array.datatype,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: we will get this as a NumPy array and we have data type object.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: Basically, in the list,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: because heterogeneous values can be stored, but NumPy array cannot.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, if we store such heterogeneous values,

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: NumPy automatically converts them into object type.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: It will not say that string, floating point, and other values are stored separately.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: It will just say that different objects are stored in it.

chunks.title: Common
chunks.text: So, this is how we use different data types in NumPy.
